sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=55, col=36).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 49, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking button bars (row 42) or stems (rows 44-46) toggles B↔w. Clicking shapes/gap/blue character/white area/checkerboard/blue bar/between-buttons has no effect (VERIFIED steps 1-26).'}
- Object taxonomy: {'checkerboard': 'B and c alternating 4x4 blocks forming background on white canvas', 'yellow_top': '14px Y shape at rows 13-16, cols 30-33 with c-colored 2px gap at row 16 cols 31-32', 'yellow_bottom': '16px Y U-shape at rows 33-37, cols 29-34', 'indicator_buttons': '5 toggle buttons at row 42, cols 20-22, 25-27, 30-32, 35-37, 40-42 with vertical stems at rows 44-46', 'blue_character': 'Blue object at bottom bounds [51,32]-[59,40], 69px', 'timer_bar': 'Blue (left) + G gray (right) at row 1, shrinks with actions'}
- HUD map: {'top_blue_bar': 'Timer/progress bar at row 1'}
Current level:
- World model: All buttons currently w. Exhaustively tested clicking: shapes, gap, blue character, white areas, checkerboard cells, blue bar, between-buttons. Only buttons themselves change state (B↔w toggle).
- Goal model: Unknown. Must find interaction that completes level. All direct clicking exhausted. Need to reconsider puzzle logic - perhaps button sequence order matters, or need to click buttons in pattern matching yellow shapes.
- Recent findings: ['G is timer bar remainder (not gameplay)', 'Clicking between buttons (row 42, col 23) had no effect', 'Only interactive elements are the 5 toggle buttons']
- Open questions: ['Does button click ORDER matter (not just final state)?', 'Is there a pattern derived from yellow shapes that maps to 5 buttons?', "Could the blue character be a 'submit' that activates after correct button config?", 'What about clicking on white border around checkerboard (rows 8-9, cols 13-14)?']
- Plan: Try clicking white border around checkerboard, then try button sequences in specific orders
- Strategy log: ['Shape clicking: no effect - abandoned', 'Gap clicking: no effect - abandoned', 'Blue char clicking: no effect - abandoned', 'Button toggling (individual, pairs, all-B, all-w): no completion', 'Matching buttons to stem colors: no completion', 'White area between board and buttons: no effect', 'Checkerboard cell clicking: no effect', 'Blue timer bar clicking: no effect', 'Between-buttons clicking: no effect']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(35,31): no change (step 1)', 'MOUSE(15,31): no change (step 2)', 'MOUSE(16,31): no change (step 3)', 'MOUSE(13,31): no change (step 4)', 'MOUSE(16,31) gap: no change (step 9)', 'MOUSE(55,36) blue char: no change (step 10)', 'All pairs matching: no completion', 'All-B config: no completion', 'All-w config: no completion', 'MOUSE(40,31) white area: no change (step 23)', 'MOUSE(25,31) checkerboard: no change (step 24)', 'MOUSE(1,31) blue bar: no change (step 25)', 'MOUSE(42,23) between buttons: no change (step 26)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 48 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Blue character click still had no effect. I'm at 48 actions and haven't solved level 1.
Let me completely rethink this puzzle. I've been assuming the puzzle is about button/stem patterns, but maybe I'm wrong about the fundamental mechanic.
Let me look at the current image more carefully:
- Checkerboard with two yellow shapes
- 5 buttons (B,B,B,w,w) with 5 stems (w,w,w,B,B)
- Blue character at bottom
- Timer bar at top
What if the puzzle is about the CHECKERBOARD itself? The checkerboard has B and c alternating blocks. What if I need to click on specific checkerboard cells to change their color, and the buttons/stems control WHICH cells can be clicked?
Or what if the puzzle is about matching the button/stem pattern to something on the checkerboard?
Wait, let me reconsider the yellow shapes. They're both yellow (Y color). What if the puzzle is about:
1. The top shape represents a "target" pattern
2. The bottom shape represents the "current" pattern
3. The buttons control how to transform one to the other
Or maybe the puzzle is simpler than I think. What if:
- The buttons need to match the yellow shape's COLUMN pattern (not row pattern)?
- Top shape has 4 columns: cols 30-33
- Bottom shape has 6 columns: cols 29-34
Let me think about what patterns the shapes could encode for 5 buttons:
Top shape (4x4):
- Column 30: all Y → B
- Column 31: Y,Y,Y,c → w (has gap)
- Column 32: Y,Y,Y,c → w (has gap)
- Column 33: all Y → B
That's 4 values, not 5.
Bottom shape (6x5):
- Column 29: Y,Y,Y,Y,Y → B
- Column 30: Y,Y,Y,Y,Y → B
- Column 31: B,B,B,Y,Y → mixed
- Column 32: B,B,B,Y,Y → mixed
- Column 33: Y,Y,Y,Y,Y → B
- Column 34: Y,Y,Y,Y,Y → B
That's 6 values, not 5.
Hmm, neither directly maps to 5 buttons by columns.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the puzzle is about clicking on the WHITE BORDER around the checkerboard? I haven't tried the corners or edges of the white area.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the image more carefully. The white area between the checkerboard and the buttons... I tried row 40, col 31. What about other positions in that white area?
Actually, I realize I should try something I haven't tried yet: clicking on the white border that SURROUNDS the checkerboard. The checkerboard is on a white canvas, so there's white space around it. Let me try clicking on the white border at different positions.
Or... what if the puzzle involves clicking on the CHECKERBOARD cells themselves, but only after setting buttons to a specific pattern?
Let me try a radical approach: click on a checkerboard cell now that buttons and stems are set to the shape pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# 
# New hypothesis: Maybe clicking checkerboard cells works AFTER 
# correct button/stem config. Let me try clicking a checkerboard cell.
# Also try clicking white border around checkerboard.

# First try clicking on white border (left edge of checkerboard area)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 15}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking button bars (row 42) toggles button B↔w. Clicking stems (rows 44-46) toggles stem B↔w INDEPENDENTLY. Blue character, shapes, gap, checkerboard, white area have no effect (VERIFIED)."}, "object_taxonomy": {"yellow_top": "4x4 Y block with 2px c gap", "yellow_bottom": "U-shape Y with bridge", "indicator_buttons": "5 toggle buttons at row 42", "blue_character": "Not interactive", "timer_bar": "Blue + G gray at row 1"}, "hud_map": {"top_blue_bar": "Timer bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Buttons B,B,B,w,w; Stems w,w,w,B,B. All previous configs failed.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to find interaction that completes level", "recent_findings": ["Shape-encoded pattern didn't complete level", "Blue character not a submit button"], "open_questions": ["Could white border be interactive?", "Do checkerboard cells become clickable after correct config?"], "current_plan": "Try white border, then checkerboard cells with current config", "strategy_log": ["All button/stem configs failed", "Blue character not submit", "Testing white border hypothesis"], "failed_probes": ["All button-only configs", "Shape pattern hypothesis", "Blue character clicks"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
